speaker: interviewer
text: Mentioned at the end of the last tape that your distant cousin in North Carolina was able to send you the $280 you needed, sent it to Paris. Help me understand how much $280 was worth at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was more than a small fortune, at that time. It-- it was worth, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in today's terms, at least $4,000-- the purchasing power, that kind of money. And for Europe, for people, who were practically stranded, who-- who were doomed, it was just like a lifeline. That's what it was for me. It was a-- it was just the difference between survival and doom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got the visa. I also got a-- a steamship ticket. Now, how did this logistically work? How much time are we talking about, here?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, we're talking about November 1938, right after the Kristallnacht. While I was in Berlin, fleeing from the Nazis in Breslau, I tried to make as much use of-- of my time, in practical terms, trying to find some way of getting out of Germany as I could. And there I had found that the Palestine and Orient Lloyd, which was a Jewish travel bureau, was-- offered people the possibility of getting out of Europe, if they had enough money for visas to South America. And that's how I landed in that office, and I got-- I was told what I had to do in order to get the visa. It was a tourist visa. You had to buy round-trip ticket because otherwise, people would never let you on board a ship. And so that's what I did. And that place was full, that office in Berlin. I still remember it. It was next to the Zionist headquarters. There was-- you couldn't even say standing room only. Most people didn't have the money or didn't have the connection that I had at the time or were turned down by their relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I finally-- I got the-- I got a passport. In fact, getting my passport, I had to apply to the passport office in Breslau of the Gestapo. There was an old man there who handed me my passport, and he said, you are one of the lucky ones. And I shuddered. I said, what are you talking about? He said, I wear the Nazi badge, but I'm one of Jehovah's witnesses. He said, you will survive, and we won't. So you had people like that once in a while. Some people had guts. So I got the-- the visa. I bought a steamship ticket. That steamship ticket also had to be paid for in dollars.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a friend from my earliest school years who had emigrated to Holland, to [INAUDIBLE], Eindhoven, as an engineer. And he got the Dutch Jewish Committee to help me finance my steamship ticket. I was about-- Then I left Germany on the 20th of December, 1938. I still remember standing there on the platform of the railroad station, saying goodbye to my family-- my mother and my fiance, about whom I want to talk in a little while.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came to the platform?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came, yes. Sure. They took me to the-- and the Gestapo had previously-- or the customs opened my suitcase, to make sure that I wasn't gonna take anything more than the clothes on my back. So I still see them disappear as the train rolled on to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in Berlin. And I had-- this friend in Holland had asked me-- since the boat that I was going to take would stop in Antwerp on its way from Hamburg, he said, why don't you come to-- to Holland, spend a few days with me, then I'll take you to the boat in Antwerp? He was about an hour away from there. So I-- I decided to do that. And so I was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had booked a ticket on the track train from Berlin to Amsterdam, to leave Berlin that very night. I arrived in Berlin, went to the Dutch embassy, and asked whether I needed a transit visa to Holland. No, you have a visitor's visa. So I took the train, the night train, to Amsterdam. And I gave a sigh of relief as we crossed the Dutch border.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few minutes later, the train stopped and the Dutch border police came in and collected passports. So my passport came right back, and he said, we can't let you into Holland. I said, you can't let me into Holland? No. You have a tourist visa only. I said-- you have no transit visa, they said. I said, but I checked with your embassy, and they said you don't need it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yes. Just about 30 minutes ago, we got a decree-- orders from Den Haag-- the Hague-- our government-- that no emigrant with tourist visa is permitted into Holland, because they may not be able to land overseas, and then we would have to take them back. He said, however, you have to get off the train. We'll give you two days. Maybe you have some people in Holland who can help you. My friend was unable to help me. He, himself, was a refugee. And after two days, they shipped me back to Germany, into the waiting arms of the Gestapo, at Osnabruck-- O-S-N-A-B-R-U-C-K. And there, the Gestapo was waiting for me. They said, you know we are going to ship you straight to a concentration camp. You came back, and that's it. I've never talked as fast in my life as I talked then. I knew my life was in the balance. I said, I had a-- I have a steamship ticket, here, on a boat that will leave Hamburg within two days. Hamburg was just two hours away, by train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how do we know? I said, you check with the steamship company. And again, there was somebody that they had to call in order to make a decision on my fate. And it was midnight. That person was not in. And I remember a third guy coming in and asking what this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brouhaha was all about. And they said, he is a Jew. We want to ship him to concentration camp. So he said, how do you come back to Germany? I explained it to him. He says, get the hell out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five minutes, I disappeared-- took the next train to Hamburg. Two days later, I was taken to the boat by my cousin from Magdeburg, who now lives in Toronto. We went. That was my exodus from Germany. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now there were some people-- you were leaving a lot behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I left a lot behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your mother and your fiance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: I also want you to talk about your sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister had married, in the meantime. She-- I left her behind. She was to emigrate to Australia seven months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- when you mentioned that you escaped Kristallnacht with a brother-in-law, was that the brother-in-law that married your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and during Kristallnacht, where was your sister, if you brother-in-law was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was home. At that time, the Nazis did not bother the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother, yeah. She was at home with my mother. But like, I was-- now, coming to my personal life. I had, of course, in addition to my scholastic interests, a lot of other interests, and one was girls. I-- we had-- I had some very lovely girlfriends, but finally, I found one who was to be my companion for 50 years. And her name was Ursula-- U-R-S-U-L-A. That I found-- The way we met was very peculiar. You know, at the time it was 1937. The Jews were excluded from any Winter Relief, which was the winter-- German Winter Relief work, which helped the poor people, gave them-- they collected the food and they collected clothes, which was then doled out to the poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jews were excluded from that. So the Jews had to have their own winter relief fund. And they came-- and usually in August, September, there were volunteers-- Jewish volunteers-- who came to Jewish homes and said, do you have any clothes that we will give to our poor people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It so happened that on one Sunday, nobody was home. They left a ticket that they had been there. My-- my mother said next Monday, when I cut classes, she said, you better go over. There's a Jewish family across the street, and there the collecting station. I went there, and I was received by a very lovely lady there, who knew my family and engaged me in a very interesting conversation-- political-- and whom I-- she knew my family. A little while later, a few minutes later, the door opened, and a lovely young girl came in-- her daughter-- wondering what her mother was doing-- who she was talking to. And she was drawn into the conversation, and I was drawn towards her. She looked beautiful, and she seemed to be a very intelligent person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after we got through, I delivered everything. She said, do you belong to the Zionist movement? I said, yes. She said, well, I'm not so convinced of that. So I said, well, can we continue our discussion sometime later? And she said, well, if you are interested. So we continued our discussion on some other date. And afterwards, I said, could we also meet privately, socially? She was, obviously, shaken. But she-- and she was not too enthused. But, in my life, I have seldom taken no for an answer, and so I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she went to school. She went to the-- she had gone to a public-- to a gymnasium. And, since Jews couldn't go to school anymore, to public school, she went to the Jewish high school. And I have her report card-- her-- her diploma here. And I tried to help her, because she had not studied Hebrew. I tried to coach her in Hebrew and in French. And usually, when I did it, her grades went down. So anyway, she always accused me, for the rest of her life, of not being-- sticking to the agenda.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I-- I was very much in love with her. And about-- I persisted for about one year, and then she finally-- she gave in and also seemed to be fond of me. And we-- was New Year's Day, 1937-1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in summer 1938, when I was in Berlin, after I had had that experience on the Baltic, she was in Berlin visiting her aunt. And we went out-- went to the forests and had a wonderful time, and we became secretly engaged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1938, on the Kristallnacht, I went over-- her I- I must say this. Her father was not very happy. He always felt that his daughter was too young. Her mother, however, was on my side. She liked me, and she knew my family. So anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she, at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ursula was born-- she was over 17-- between 17 and 18. And finally, in 1938, in November, after I had booked my steamship ticket and my date-- the date of my departure had been set, we got secretly engaged. And the day before my departure, we went to a little store-- Jewelry Box, called. and I gave her a ring. And then we had to announce it to her family. And her father said, well, I still recall that things have gone to this-- this far, I might just as well say OK, and so did her mother. And the next day, Ursula joined my family in taking me to the railroad station. We didn't know when we would see each other again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have concerns that you might not see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She still went to school. She did not graduate until April 1939, and we didn't know where she would go. I didn't know whether I could stay in Peru. My fate was uncertain, and so was hers, but we decided we were going to keep in correspondence, and if and when opportunity would permit, we would get together again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about trying to find a visa for her, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but at that time, she had to finish school. She then went-- she took her exam-- passed the exam in April 1939. And then filed for admission to England. Because some people had been able to go to England-- got a visa. And she wanted to go out as a domestic, just to work in England for a household. And one day in June 1939, she was called by the British Consulate General in-- in Berlin. They had the visa for her. And so she left on the last-- in August 1939, on the last crossing of the from Germany to Southampton, last crossing before World War II broke out. She went-- she worked for a family there. And I was able, then-- I'm taking a big jump--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why don't we come back to where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- do you have any details about how she was able to arrange that passage? She must have been very lucky, to get that job.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very lucky to get--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it just the luck of the draw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was luck-- it was good luck, and the-- the Jewish Committee in London helped her with that job. She was-- she had tremendous difficulties in being admitted to England, because the British passport control in Southampton didn't want to admit her. They said, you're barely 18. You couldn't have entered into a valid contract, and we can't admit you to Great Britain. And she just talked-- must have talked as fast as I did to the Gestapo, to convince the British passport control to let her land in Southampton. She went to the Bloomsbury House, it was called at the time. That is the Jewish Relief Committee. And they arranged for a job with a Belgian family, a family whose-- the husband worked for Reuters, for the British agency. And she stayed there, in London about four months until I arranged for marriage by proxy, but that is another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get to that. So so you were off to Peru.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was off to Peru.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a problem, landing in Peru. And I don't-- can't go into detail on this, but the Peruvian government got wind, through a passenger of my boat, who-- that the visas on which we were sailing for Peru had been obtained in Paris. And that Paris visas for German residents should-- should be invalidated, because the competent Peruvian authority was the consul general in Berlin, who I understood was a great anti-Semite and wouldn't have given us any visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the office-- the-- not the steamship office, but the travel office in Berlin had persuaded the consul general in Paris for a, shall we say, consideration to issue visas to German residents. We were on the boat. And the story broke in Peru. And when we came to-- in fact, I can say it was a passenger, who-- who had visited the consul-- the Peruvian consul-- in Belgium, and the consul had noticed his visa there, and he said, that's irregular.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came on board-- the Peruvian consul in Antwerp came on board our ship and told the captain not to permit us to sail any further, and the-- because our visas were invalid. The captain told him to get the hell off the boat, because he was carrying passengers. According to his instructions, they had paid both ways. But Peru was warned, and we had tremendous difficulties, landing in Peru. I first landed in Baltimore, where I met a-- I-- I gave an interview to the I was the only English-speaking person on board that vessel. And then New York, and we understood-- we tried to do our best. We sent a radio wire to the Jewish Community and the National Council of Jewish Women, in New York. They got a hold of the-- some humanitarians in the United States, one of whom was Eleanor Roosevelt, who helped with the Peruvian authorities, I understood, behind the scenes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, finally, we were not permitted to land in the main port of Peru-- which is Callao-- C-A-L-L-A-O-- but in the southernmost port, Mollendo-- M-O-L-L-E-N-D-O, which was the backwaters. And the customs officials, as I understand, some of them couldn't even read. So they let us land there. And, from there, I went-- we went to a little city by the name of Arequipa, A-R-E-Q-U-I-P-A, where, after five days, I got a job with a British import-export company. Due to my knowledge of Spanish and English, I was able to get a job. And it belonged-- the company belonged to the vice president of Peru, who took a liking to me and had me teach a few courses at the university. I'm giving this in short order.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was also a Jewish-- a little Jewish congregation there and-- I got-- I enjoyed living there. I was able to keep body and soul together. I didn't make an money, but it was enjoyable. A few weeks after the arrival, my arrival, and that of other Jewish refugees, the Catholic paper published a big story about Jews arriving there and contaminating the Catholic world, and they had been expelled for the misdeeds that they did, and so on, and so forth. It was very unpleasant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, as a matter of fact, a lot of people said, well, you cannot be Jews. I said, why not? Well, you don't have any horns. And I'm serious. I said, that's impossible. You are kidding us. And we told them who we were. It was just incomprehensible. They had been taught that the Jews are cursed and you have to avoid them. So I stayed there, nevertheless. It was a-- [LAUGHS] despite all of that, it was a pleasant stay until things got very, very critical in Europe. Everybody knew, in mid-1939, that the war was a matter of month-- how do I get my family out of Germany, my mother-- my-- my prospective in-laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ursula had, in the meantime, gone to England. And my mother wrote to me that they may have to go to Shanghai. It was the last resort. I did what I could. I had their passports shipped to Peru-- but it didn't-- in order to get visas, it was impossible. In-- meanwhile, a friend of mine had, like quite a few other refugees, had- had landed in Bolivia, which was adjacent to Peru, just across Lake Tititcaca. And he said, well, if you're lucky you might be able to help your folks from here. So I pulled up stakes in Peru, crossed Lake Titicaca, and settled down in La Paz. After a few hours-- a few-- few weeks, I was able to get a-- an audience with the president of Bolivia, Quintanilla, through the recommendations of my landlady's son, who had gone to school with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bolivia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bolivia. And he said he was-- must have been impressed by my Spanish. He said, OK, remarkable you are one of the few who speak Spanish. And I told him that I like the language of Cervantes, and I enjoyed very much being in Bolivia and finding a new homeland there, and that I wished for my folks to join me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he looked at me, and then he called-- he pressed a button. He said, take this gentleman to my secretary. I want him to give me-- I want him to give you the names of his people, and the visa is granted. So I was able to get that. Then-- Were you only able to give the names of people for whom you already had the passports, because they had shipped them to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the passports had to be shipped back to Germany. Because, in the meanwhile, the steamship companies had all been warned against getting-- taking passengers on board whose visas had not been issued in Germany. And these visas would not have been issued in Germany. So I have to pay a lot of money for these visas. They were completely worthless.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually did obtain visas for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- well, I borrowed. I borrowed money. I went in Bolivia, I went to the relief committee, and I said, we need-- I need some money here for that. But that is another story, because Quintanilla, like most other Bolivian presidents, was kicked out. You know, in Bolivia, no president up until decades ago has ever served out his term. In the meantime, coming back, going back to my personal experiences, my personal life-- Ursula had landed in England. And I was trying to get her to Bolivia. And in Bolivia you couldn't get any female into the country-- unattached female-- unless he or she was accompanied by an adult-- relative or parents, or married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saw a very resourceful lawyer who said, but we have a law here which permits marriage by proxy. We send her a power of attorney to be filled out before the Bolivian consul general. The power of attorney then requires somebody whom she should elect as her attorney in fact in Bolivia to appear before the notary public, sign it, and then she is married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Ursula had an aunt in Bolivia who had also immigrated. And she became-- she was given the-- the power. And that's how I arranged for her to get the visa. She appeared about two months later. Now, Bolivia is about 4,000 meters high-- about 13,000 feet. She didn't-- Ursula didn't like the climate-- didn't agree with her. But we had no choice, anyway. And then, in the meantime--

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been very happy to see her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were-- we were very happy to get together. Can you imagine after? And she crossed the Atlantic, after the war had broken out. One of the boats-- it was a convoy-- that accompanied her was sunk by the German U-boats in the North Atlantic. I remember she said the happiest moment was when they reached Panama. She crossed the Panama Canal, and then, of course, she went down the Pacific Ocean. But a few months later, after her arrival, we were trying to go her parents out. All visas had to be reconfirmed, and the president, in the meantime, was out of power. They couldn't leave Germany. And we went-- Ursula was of great help. She and another friend of hers went to the-- see the-- the deputy chief of immigration and talked him into granting a special permission to her parents. It was 1940. The parents and my mother had thought they could emigrate by way of Genoa, Italy. The Italian steamship companies plied from between Italy and-- and South America. Italy entered the war, so all shipping across the Atlantic ceased. A way was found. We borrowed the money. The Jewish committee had found that it was possible for people-- Jews going to the United States or elsewhere in the western hemisphere-- to have them-- to arrange for transportation by rail. They took the train in Berlin, went to Riga, Moscow, where they stayed for one week. then took the Trans Siberian Express all the way to Vladivostok. Took the boat, Vladivostok, to Korea, Busan. From Korea, a ferry took them to Japan-- Shimonoseki. They took the Japanese boat, the in Kobe. Went across the Pacific, landed in Honolulu, where they were-- they go their first decent meal. From Honolulu to Los Angeles, where they were not permitted to leave the ship, and then down the west coast of South America, to Arica, Chile because Bolivia is landlocked. And then from Chile to La Paz, where we saw them in November 1940. They had left Berlin during the British bombardment in August 1940. So around the world in 80 days is no saga. They lived with us-- they and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we stop right here, and we'll change tapes.
